management_notes:
Patrick Brandl was appointed Interim President and CEO on August 1, 2025, replacing founding CEO George Sookochoff who resigned effective July 31, 2025. Brandl holds a master's degree in law and business administration and is a Senior Partner at bgm Partners (Vienna/Luzern) — a legal and advisory background rather than operational mining experience. He represents HMS Bergbau AG, a global commodities firm that is one of the company's largest shareholders following the April 2025 private placement.

Three European institutional investors — HMS Bergbau AG (HMS), ERAG Energie & Rohstoff AG (ERAG), and LaVo Verwaltungsgesellschaft MBH (LaVo) — became combined Control Persons upon completion of the April 2025 financing. This concentration creates alignment between management and the dominant shareholder group but raises governance concerns for minority shareholders. No SEDI open-market share purchases by management were identified during this research cycle, and no confirmed CFO appointment was found in available disclosures.

Technical continuity is provided by Laurence Sookochoff, P.Eng., who serves as Technical Advisor and is the NI 43-101 Qualified Person for all exploration programs. The overall management structure is in mid-transition: an interim CEO less than one year into the role, unclear CFO status, and a board composition tilted toward the European control group. This instability earns a below-average score.
geology_notes:
Belmont holds a multi-project portfolio — Come By Chance (Cu-Au, British Columbia), Athelstan-Jackpot (Au, British Columbia), and Crackingstone (U-REE, Saskatchewan) — but none carry a current NI 43-101 compliant mineral resource estimate. The company is firmly in the early exploration stage across all flagship assets.

The 2025 diamond drilling program at Come By Chance (5 holes, 2,000+ meters) was completed but did not intersect economic mineralization. The company described outcomes as enhancing 'geological understanding' of the structural and alteration system — standard disclosure framing for a negative drill campaign. The Athelstan-Jackpot project hosts two historically productive mines (7,600 oz Au + 9,000 oz Ag combined historical production) with a 1,500-metre gold trend; preliminary trenching and sampling suggest a potential surface resource of only 2,000–5,000 oz gold — an extremely small target by any junior mining standard. The Crackingstone project offers the most compelling geological signal: historic surface samples up to 15.6% U3O8, historical small-scale mining at 2.3% U3O8 average grade, and three major structural corridors totaling 10 km identified by modern geophysics.

The absence of any NI 43-101 resource at any project, combined with the failed 2025 Come By Chance drill campaign at the most advanced asset, constrains this score to 2. Crackingstone's historical grades and district-scale structural corridors are the only meaningful geological upside signal in the portfolio.
capital_notes:
As of June 2025, Belmont had approximately 106 million common shares issued and outstanding following partial completion of the April 2025 private placement, which raised gross proceeds of $1,363,500 by issuing 30.3 million shares at $0.045 per share — a deeply dilutive offering at the lowest pricing tier common on the TSXV. Earlier in the company's history, management sought shareholder approval for a 3:1 share consolidation, a clear signal that share price has been chronically depressed and that the structure required correction. Pre-consolidation share counts were in excess of 100 million.

Net proceeds from the April 2025 financing were earmarked as follows: $1.1M for exploration and drilling across the project portfolio, $75K for management fees, and approximately $150K for working capital, legal, accounting, and wire services. With multiple planned drill programs across three properties, the treasury provides a limited runway of approximately 12–18 months before additional financing will be required. One structural positive: no warrants were outstanding as of the most recently available financial statements, avoiding a common warrant overhang problem seen in many TSXV-stage explorers.

The repeated pattern of low-price share issuance, prior consolidation attempt, and a concentrated European control group (which reduces effective public float and retail liquidity) warrants a below-average capital score. The small treasury and multi-project ambition create a mismatch that will likely require further dilutive financing within the current field season.
catalyst_notes:
The most concrete near-term catalyst is the planned 2026 drill program at the Crackingstone Uranium–REE Project in Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin. Belmont received the Crown Land Work Authorization (drill permit) from the Saskatchewan Ministry of Environment in December 2025, valid through November 2028. As of the most recent disclosures, the company was finalizing drill contractor selection, access route optimization, and the final 2026 drill layout integrating geological, radiometric, magnetic, and structural data layers. A Q2 2026 field program commencement is plausible given the permit status and planning stage.

At the Athelstan-Jackpot Gold Project, Q3 2025 drilling was planned to target southwest-trending linear high-resistivity zones interpreted as potential feeder structures from the Jackpot fault. Results from this campaign were not found in any source reviewed during this research, leaving uncertainty about whether drilling was completed, delayed, or yielded results not yet disclosed as of April 2026.

A score of 3 reflects genuine permitted activity at Crackingstone and the possibility of Athelstan-Jackpot drill results entering the news cycle, offset by the Come By Chance 2025 program's failure to intersect economic mineralization and no confirmed assay results pending at any project. No production decision, feasibility study, or PEA is in scope for any property.
acquisition_notes:
With no NI 43-101 compliant mineral resource estimate at any flagship project, a formal P/NAV calculation is not possible and NAV per share cannot be determined. Belmont's enterprise value is estimated at approximately CAD $3.0–4.4M, derived from a market capitalization of approximately $5.9M at the $0.045 share price and adjusting for an estimated $1–1.5M cash position from the April 2025 financing net proceeds. The company is effectively valued on exploration optionality and land position rather than any defined in-ground resource.

For comparative context, early-stage gold explorer transactions on the TSXV have historically valued pre-resource projects between $2–10M EV depending on jurisdiction, commodity, and exploration data quality. At $3–4M EV, Belmont is at the low end of this range. If the Crackingstone project were to define a uranium resource, comparables would be drawn from Athabasca Basin uranium transactions, where high-grade deposits have commanded significant premiums; however, uranium transaction comparables are sparse and highly grade-dependent, and no resource exists on which to base such a comparison.

Until at least one project achieves a formal resource estimate, the acquisition value score is anchored near the floor. The low enterprise value could represent deep value if any project delivers a discovery, but the 2025 Come By Chance campaign's negative result materially reduces confidence in a near-term resource at the flagship copper-gold asset.
analyst_summary:
Belmont Resources Inc. (BEA.V) receives an AVOID verdict with a composite score of 11/25. The company is a pre-resource, multi-commodity explorer — gold, copper, uranium, and REE — with no NI 43-101 compliant resource estimate at any of its three flagship projects. The most material near-term negative is the 2025 Come By Chance diamond drilling campaign, which completed over 2,000 metres across 5 holes and failed to intersect economic mineralization at the company's most advanced asset in British Columbia.

Management is in active transition, with an interim CEO (Patrick Brandl, appointed August 2025) drawn from a legal and advisory background rather than operational mining. The capital structure has been chronically diluted through repeated low-price financings, and the prior pursuit of a 3:1 share consolidation is a structural red flag. With over 106 million shares outstanding at $0.045 and a market cap of approximately $6M CAD, Belmont has severely limited financial resources to advance multiple exploration programs simultaneously. No open-market insider buying was identified, and governance is complicated by a European control group holding a combined controlling position — a structure that may not be aligned with Canadian retail investor interests.

The key catalyst to watch is the 2026 drill program at the Crackingstone Uranium–REE Project in Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin, for which a drill permit was received in December 2025. Historic surface samples up to 15.6% U3O8 and 10 kilometres of identified structural corridors give this project legitimate exploration merit. A positive initial drill intersection at Crackingstone — particularly high-grade uranium within a structural corridor — would be a significant re-rating event and would prompt an immediate reassessment of this verdict. Until drilling delivers results, the risk/reward at $0.045 does not justify a position.
